summary: DeVault argues that coding style debates are a waste of time and the only principle that matters is conformity to existing conventions. Use whatever is standard for the language you're working in, and when contributing to someone else's project, follow their style without complaint. The post mocks the passion people bring to tabs-vs-spaces debates while acknowledging personal preferences are irrelevant to the principle. He makes one exception to rant about aligned-to-opening-paren function parameter formatting, which he considers genuinely bad. The core message is pragmatic: pick the most common style, enforce it consistently, and stop arguing about it.
key_points: The only coding style principle that matters is conformity — use whatever is standard for the language
When contributing to someone else's project, follow their conventions without complaint
Having a consistent style guide matters far more than which specific style you choose
Aligned-to-opening-paren parameter formatting wastes horizontal space and forces mixing tabs and spaces
Personal preferences about tabs vs spaces are irrelevant — use what the ecosystem expects
money_quotes: There's only one principle that matters in coding style: don't rock the boat.
Just do whatever the most common thing is in the language you're working in.
This hill isn't worth dying on.
Don't get snippy about their coding style. Just follow their style guide, and if there isn't one, just make your code look like the code around it.
It's none of your goddamn business how they choose to style their code.
Which style guide you use doesn't matter nearly as much as using one.
For the record, tabs are objectively better. Does that mean I'm going to write my JavaScript with tabs? Hell no!
key_insight: Coding style debates are a waste of energy — the only rule that matters is to follow whatever convention is already established for the language or project you're working in.
